Nanyang Primary School — 2024

Primary 6 Preliminary Examination — Science — Total: 100 marks (Booklet A: 56 marks, Booklet B: 44 marks) — 1 h 45 min

Booklet A — Multiple Choice Questions (56 marks, Questions 1–28, 2 marks each)

Question 1

2 marks

Which of the following describe(s) both the field habitat and the garden habitat correctly?

A   Both habitats can have some common animal populations living in them.
B   The plants in both habitats provide food and shelter for some of the animals living there.
C   Flowering plants can only be found in the garden habitat while non-flowering plants can only be found in the field habitat.

  1. A and B only
  2. A and C only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 3 — B and C only. B is correct: plants in both habitats provide food and shelter for animals. C is correct in this context: the field habitat is characterised by non-flowering plants (grasses, ferns) while the garden habitat is cultivated with flowering plants. A is incorrect because the two habitats typically support different animal populations.

Question 2

2 marks

Nora saw bird X in a garden. Thirty minutes later, she concluded that bird X forms a population in the garden community. Which of the following observations is/are needed to make the conclusion?

A   Bird X eating earthworms
B   Bird X feeding two of its young
C   Bird X perching on its predator
D   Bird X building its nest with its male

  1. A and B only
  2. A and C only
  3. B and D only
  4. A, B and D

Answer

Option 3 — B and D only. A population is a group of organisms of the same species living in the same habitat at the same time. B (bird X feeding its young) shows there are young X birds in the garden too. D (bird X building its nest with its male) shows there is another bird X (the male) in the same garden. Both observations indicate multiple bird X organisms in the garden, supporting a population. A and C do not provide evidence of other X birds.

Question 3

2 marks

Study the food web below and answer Questions 3 and 4.

Which of the following statements about the food web is false?

  1. Energy in organism E is transferred to organism D.
  2. Energy in organism B cannot be transferred to organism C.
  3. Organisms B and C compete for the same food source.
  4. Organisms A, B, C, D and E belong to the same community.

Answer

Option 1. In the food web, arrows point from prey to predator (showing energy flow). Organism D is eaten by organism E (D→E), so energy flows from D to E. Therefore the statement “energy in organism E is transferred to organism D” is false. Options 2, 3 and 4 are all true: B and C both eat A (same food source), there is no path for B’s energy to reach C directly, and all five organisms coexist in the same habitat.

Question 4

2 marks

(Refer to the food web in Question 3.)

Which of the following changes in population size could be observed after an increase in organism E’s population size?

  1. Increase in organism A’s population size
  2. Increase in organism B’s population size
  3. Increase in organism D’s population size
  4. Decrease in organism C’s population size

Answer

Option 2. If E increases → more D is eaten → D decreases → less B and C are eaten → B (and C) increase. Organism A would decrease (more B and C eating it). D decreases (more eaten by E). C increases (not decreases).

Question 5

2 marks

Which of the food webs below shows three populations of predators?

Answer

Option 1. In food web (1), organisms U, R and S are top predators — they each eat other organisms but are not eaten by any organism in the food web. That gives exactly three populations of predators (U, R and S). The other food webs each have fewer than three such top predators.

Question 6

2 marks

Which of the following examples of adaptations had been grouped correctly?

StructuralBehavioural
(1)sharp clawssmall and light body
(2)long tail finswebbed feet
(3)body patternsproducing distinct sounds
(4)hunting in groupsusing venom to kill prey

Answer

Option 3. Body patterns are physical features of an organism → structural adaptation. Producing distinct sounds (e.g. warning calls) is an action performed by the organism → behavioural adaptation. In option (1), “small and light body” is structural, not behavioural. In option (2), both are structural. In option (4), “hunting in groups” is behavioural, not structural.

Question 7

2 marks

Which of the following adaptations of plants is correctly matched with its purpose?

AdaptationPurpose
(1)Needle-like leaves of a cactusTo store water for the plant
(2)Hairy reproductive parts that hang out of a flowerTo trap pollen in the air easily
(3)Spongy leaf stalks filled with airTo allow the plant to float on water
(4)Fruits of a plant are brightly-colouredTo attract pollinators

Answer

Option 3. Spongy leaf stalks filled with air provide buoyancy, allowing aquatic plants to float on water. Option (1) is wrong — needle-like leaves reduce water loss, not store water (water is stored in the stem). Option (2) is wrong — hanging stamens release pollen into the air, not trap it. Option (4) is wrong — brightly-coloured fruits attract animals that eat the fruit and disperse seeds, not pollinators.

Question 8

2 marks

Which of the following adaptations help animals to avoid being spotted by their predators?

A   Changing body colour to green when resting on a green leaf
B   Changing fur colour from brown to white during the snowy winter
C   A non-poisonous animal having the appearance of a poisonous animal
D   Having body shapes and colour that look like branches on the plant they live on

  1. A only
  2. B and C only
  3. A, B and D only
  4. A, C and D only

Answer

Option 3 — A, B and D only. A, B and D are all forms of camouflage that help the animal blend into its surroundings, making it harder for predators to spot it. C (mimicry of a poisonous animal) deters predators from attacking rather than preventing detection — the predator can still see the animal; it just chooses not to eat it.

Question 9

2 marks

Which of the following actions will help to conserve our natural resources?

A   Using more paper bags instead of plastic bags
B   Taking a bus instead of a car to work
C   Clearing forests by burning trees
D   Recycling used materials

  1. A and B only
  2. A and D only
  3. B and D only
  4. A, B and D only

Answer

Option 3 — B and D only. B (public transport) reduces fuel consumption; D (recycling) reduces the need to extract new raw materials — both conserve natural resources. A uses more trees (paper comes from trees); C destroys natural resources by burning forests. Neither A nor C conserves resources.

Question 10

2 marks

The diagram below shows a river. The arrow indicates the flow of river water.

The graph below shows the number of fish over time at part Y of the river.

Which of the following could have happened at part X of the river bank to cause the change in the number of fish?

A   Picking up litter
B   Planting more trees
C   Discharge of factory waste into the river
D   Spraying of pesticides regularly on plants growing by the river

  1. A and B only
  2. A and D only
  3. B and C only
  4. C and D only

Answer

Option 4 — C and D only. The graph shows the fish population at Y decreasing over time. C (factory waste discharged into the river) and D (pesticides sprayed on riverbank plants washing into the river) are pollutants that harm or kill fish, causing the decrease. A (picking up litter) and B (planting more trees) are conservation measures that would help the fish population, not reduce it.

Question 11

2 marks

The diagram below shows a flower that has developed into a fruit. Part X can germinate into a new plant.

Which statement(s) is/are correct?

A   Pollination had taken place.
B   Part X developed from an ovule.
C   The flower has many ovules in the ovary.

  1. B only
  2. A and B only
  3. A and C only
  4. B and C only

Answer

Option 2 — A and B only. A is correct: for a fruit and seed to form, pollination (and subsequent fertilisation) must have taken place. B is correct: Part X can germinate, so Part X is a seed, which developed from an ovule. C is incorrect: the diagram shows only one seed/ovule, so we cannot conclude the flower has many ovules.

Question 12

2 marks

The diagram below shows cells P and Q during process H in the reproduction of an animal.

Which one of the following correctly identifies cell P, cell Q and process H?

Cell PCell QProcess H
(1)eggspermfertilisation
(2)eggspermpollination
(3)spermeggfertilisation
(4)spermeggpollination

Answer

Option 1. P is the larger cell = egg cell; Q is the smaller cell = sperm cell. H is the process where sperm and egg join = fertilisation (not pollination, which involves pollen transferring to a stigma, not a cell-to-cell union).

Question 13

2 marks

Amy wanted to find out how the temperature of water affects the rate of movement of water in stems. She used three similar pieces of stem and three identical beakers with the same volume of water. She recorded the volume of water left in the beakers after 3 hours.

Which one of the following variables should be kept constant in her experiment?

  1. length of stem
  2. temperature of water
  3. volume of water transported up each piece of stem
  4. time taken for the water to reach the top of the stem

Answer

Option 1 — length of stem. Amy is investigating how temperature affects water movement in stems. Temperature is the independent (changing) variable. The volume of water transported (3) and the time taken (4) are both dependent (measured) variables. Length of stem (1) is the variable that must be kept constant to make the test fair — she already used “three similar pieces of stem” to achieve this.

Question 14

2 marks

Read the information below and use it to answer Questions 14 and 15.

Tom has a toy shown below. When he turns the handle, the rubber band becomes twisted. The wings move up and down loudly when the handle is rotated.

Tom turned the handle 40 times and threw the toy in the air at point P. The toy flew from P to Q, then flew at the same height from Q to R before stopping at S as shown below.

Which of the following correctly shows the main energy changes in the toy when it is released at point P and flew to point Q?

Before release at PFrom P to Q
(1)kinetic energy→ sound energy
(2)kinetic energy→ potential energy + sound energy
(3)potential energy→ kinetic energy + sound energy
(4)potential energy→ kinetic energy + potential energy

Answer

Option 3. Before release at P, the wound rubber band has elastic potential energy. When the toy is released and flies from P to Q, the elastic potential energy is converted into kinetic energy (the toy moves) and sound energy (the wings buzz/whir loudly as they spin).

Question 15

2 marks

(Refer to the toy path diagram in Question 14.)

Which of the following explains why the toy flew lower from R to S than from P to Q?

A   Gravitational force acts on the toy from R to S.
B   There is less kinetic energy in the toy than before.
C   There is less potential energy stored in the rubber band than before.

  1. A only
  2. A and B only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 4 — A, B and C. A is correct: gravitational force pulls the toy downward from R to S. B is correct: by R, the toy has used up much of its kinetic energy, so it cannot travel as high. C is correct: by R, the rubber band has largely unwound and has less (or no) elastic potential energy left to power the wings upward. All three explain why the flight from R to S is lower than P to Q.

Question 16

2 marks

Which of the following organ systems are all working when you walk and run?

A   Skeletal system
B   Respiratory system
C   Circulatory system
D   Muscular system

  1. A and C only
  2. B and D only
  3. B, C and D only
  4. A, B, C and D

Answer

Option 4 — A, B, C and D. All four systems are active: muscles (D) contract and relax to move the limbs; bones and joints (A) provide support and leverage; breathing rate increases to take in more oxygen (B); and heart rate increases to pump oxygenated blood to working muscles (C).

Question 17

2 marks

The flowchart below classifies three types of forces X, Y and Z.

Which of the following correctly identifies X, Y and Z?

XYZ
(1)Frictional forceElastic spring forceGravitational force
(2)Magnetic forceFrictional forceElastic spring force
(3)Elastic spring forceMagnetic forceFrictional force
(4)Frictional forceMagnetic forceGravitational force

Answer

Option 4. X does not act from a distance (contact force) → frictional force. Y acts from a distance but not only as a pulling force (can push or pull) → magnetic force. Z acts from a distance and only involves pulling → gravitational force (gravity only attracts, never repels).

Question 18

2 marks

Emma set up an experiment as shown in the diagram below.

She hung different numbers of weights on the spring and measured the length of the spring. Then, she plotted a graph below to show the results.

Using her results, she calculated the extension of the spring.
Extension = New length of spring − original length of spring.

If the extension of the spring is 4 cm, how many weights did she use?

  1. 1
  2. 2
  3. 3
  4. 0

Answer

Option 2 — 2 weights. From the graph, the original length of the spring (0 weights) is 2 cm. Each additional weight extends the spring by 2 cm. An extension of 4 cm means: new length = 2 + 4 = 6 cm. Reading from the graph, a spring length of 6 cm corresponds to 2 weights.

Question 19

2 marks

A circuit was set up with a battery and a bulb, with two gaps at positions A and B. Different objects were placed at positions A and B and it was observed whether the bulb lit up.

Object at AObject at BBulb lit up?
JKNo
JLYes
LMYes

Which of the following correctly identifies the materials J, K, L and M?

JKLM
(1)SteelRubberIronAluminium
(2)WoodIronRubberSteel
(3)IronAluminiumSteelRubber
(4)RubberWoodAluminiumSteel

Answer

Option 1. From J+L (Yes): both J and L are conductors. From J+K (No): since J is a conductor, K must be an insulator. From L+M (Yes): M is a conductor. So: J = conductor, K = insulator, L = conductor, M = conductor. Only option (1) matches: J=steel (conductor) ✓, K=rubber (insulator) ✓, L=iron (conductor) ✓, M=aluminium (conductor) ✓.

Question 20

2 marks

Kai set up the following circuits using similar bulbs and batteries as shown in the diagram below. All the bulbs and batteries are in good working condition.

He arranged the bulbs from dimmest to brightest. Which of the following has been arranged correctly?

Brightness of bulb
Dimmest → Brightest
(1)HGEF
(2)FEGH
(3)EGFH
(4)GHFE

Answer

Option 2 — F, E, G, H (dimmest to brightest). More batteries in series and fewer bulbs in series = brighter bulbs. Circuit F has the least energy per bulb (dimmest); circuit H has the most batteries with bulbs arranged to maximise brightness (brightest). E and G fall in between.

Question 21

2 marks

Which actions below help to conserve electricity?

A   Use the fan instead of air-conditioner.
B   Switch off the lights when leaving the room.
C   Shower without using the water heater on a hot day.
D   Use the half flush instead of full flush for liquid waste.

  1. A and B only
  2. C and D only
  3. A, B and C only
  4. B, C and D only

Answer

Option 3 — A, B and C only. A (fan uses less electricity than air-conditioner), B (lights off = no wasted electricity), and C (no water heater = no electrical energy used for heating water) all conserve electricity. D (half flush) conserves water, not electricity.

Question 22

2 marks

Manfred set up an experiment with a torch, an object, a light sensor (placed on top of the object) and a screen. He recorded the shadow height and the light sensor reading as shown in the table.

ReadingShadow height (cm)Light sensor reading
110040
212540
314040
416540

Which change did Manfred make between each reading?

  1. He moved the torch nearer to the object.
  2. He moved the screen nearer to the object.
  3. He moved the object further away from the screen.
  4. He moved the screen further away from the object.

Answer

Option 4. The light sensor reading stays constant at 40 (so the torch position relative to object is unchanged), but the shadow grows taller. The only change that makes a shadow grow taller without altering the light reaching the object is moving the screen further away from the object.

Question 23

2 marks

Four containers W, X, Y and Z each hold water at different temperatures and different volumes, as shown below.

Which containers have the least and most amount of heat energy?

Least amount of heatMost amount of heat
(1)ZY
(2)XZ
(3)ZW
(4)YX

Answer

Option 3. The amount of heat depends on both the temperature and the volume (mass) of water. W (95°C, 50 ml) has both the highest temperature AND the greatest volume → most heat. Z (40°C, 10 ml) has a low temperature AND the smallest volume → least heat.

Question 24

2 marks

Four rods P, Q, R and S are each made of different materials. One end of each rod is placed in water at 100°C. A small piece of wax is attached to the other end of each rod. The time taken for the wax to melt is recorded.

RodTime for wax to melt (min)
P5
Q3
R6
S7

Chloe wants to make a box to keep her ice cubes from melting for as long as possible. Which material should she use?

  1. P
  2. Q
  3. R
  4. S

Answer

Option 4 — S. The longer the wax takes to melt, the poorer the conductor of heat. S takes the longest (7 min) → S is the poorest conductor of heat → using S slows down the transfer of heat from the surroundings into the box, keeping the ice cubes cold the longest.

Question 25

2 marks

Ray lowered an empty glass into a container of water until it touched the bottom. The small wooden ball floated as shown in the diagram below.

Which one of the following statements correctly explains the difference in water level inside and outside the glass?

  1. The air trapped in the glass occupied space.
  2. The small wooden ball in the glass occupied space.
  3. The air trapped in the glass does not have a fixed shape.
  4. The small wooden ball pushed the water inside the glass.

Answer

Option 2. The small wooden ball floating inside the glass occupies space. This displaces water inside the glass, causing the water level inside the glass to be higher than the water level outside. The ball’s volume accounts for the difference in water levels.

Question 26

2 marks

Three metal containers are each filled with substances P, Q and R. A rubber lid and a 1 kg weight are placed on top of each container as shown below.

The rubber lid of container R was pushed downwards. Which of the following correctly explains this observation?

  1. Substance P, Q and R are all liquids.
  2. Substance Q has no definite volume.
  3. Substance R is a gas and can be compressed.
  4. Substance P is a solid and Q and R are both gases.

Answer

Option 3. The 1 kg weight pushed the rubber lid of container R inward, meaning the substance inside was compressed → R must be a gas (gases can be compressed; liquids and solids cannot). The other containers’ lids did not move inward, so P and Q are not gases.

Question 27

2 marks

The diagrams below show the results when metal bars AB, CD and EF were brought close to each other in different combinations.

Which of the following correctly identifies the nature of bars AB, CD and EF?

ABCDEF
(1)MagnetMagnetic materialMagnet
(2)Magnetic materialMagnetMagnet
(3)MagnetMagnetMagnetic material
(4)MagnetMagnetMagnet

Answer

Option 4. Repulsion can only occur between two magnets (a magnet and a magnetic material can only attract, never repel). Since all three bars can repel each other in some combination shown in the diagrams, all three must be magnets.

Question 28

2 marks

The diagram below shows an electromagnet connected to two batteries. A bar magnet is attached to a spring. The bar magnet is repelled by the electromagnet, causing a pointer to indicate a reading on the scale.

One battery is removed, leaving only one battery. Which of the following describes what happens to the pointer and the strength of the electromagnet?

Pointer moves…Strength of electromagnet
(1)UpwardsIncreased
(2)UpwardsDecreased
(3)DownwardsIncreased
(4)DownwardsDecreased

Answer

Option 4. Fewer batteries → less electric current → weaker electromagnet (strength decreased). With weaker repulsion, the bar magnet is pushed up less and falls back → spring is less compressed → pointer moves downwards.

Booklet B — Open-Ended Questions (44 marks, Questions 29–40)

Question 29

5 marks

The diagram below shows a pond habitat.

Animals W and X lay eggs among the stems of plant S.

(a) State one advantage of laying eggs among the stems.  [1]

During periods of hot weather, the population of plant R increases at a faster rate and they cover the entire surface of the pond.

(b) Explain why plant S is more affected than plant Q when the population of plant R increased.  [2]

Animal X has moist skin while animal W has scales.

(c)(i) Identify the animal groups that X and W belong to.  [1]

AnimalAnimal group
X
W

(c)(ii) Based on the animal groups in (c)(i), give a reason why animal X is less affected than animal W after plants S and T are removed.  [1]

Answer

(a) The stems provide shelter/protection for the eggs from predators. Accept: Eggs are hidden among the stems so they are less likely to be found and eaten by predators. / The stems provide a safe place for the eggs to develop.

(b) Plant R covers the entire surface of the pond, blocking sunlight from reaching plant S (which is submerged/underwater). Without sunlight, plant S cannot carry out photosynthesis and cannot make food, so plant S will die. Plant Q grows on the bank (above the water surface) and is not covered by plant R, so it still receives sunlight and is less affected.

(c)(i) X: Amphibian    W: Fish

(c)(ii) X is an amphibian and can move onto land to breathe air / find food / survive, even when plants S and T are removed from the water. W is a fish and cannot leave the water, so when the underwater plants are removed (reducing shelter and food), W has fewer resources and is more severely affected.

Question 30

3 marks

The food web below shows the relationships between organisms P, Q, R, S and T.

(a) Classify organisms P, Q, R, S and T under the correct headings in the table below.  [2]

ProducerPlant-eaterAnimal-eater

There was a sudden decrease in the population of organism R even though the population of the other organisms remained the same.

(b) Give a possible reason for this decrease.  [1]

Answer

(a) Producer: P and Q    Plant-eater: T    Animal-eater: S and R

(b) Any one of: A new predator of R was introduced into the habitat. / An outbreak of disease infected and killed R. / R migrated out of the habitat. / The food source of R suddenly decreased, causing R to starve.

Question 31

4 marks

Female animal F lays its eggs on green leaves. Male animal F will guard the eggs after they are laid. Male animal F has spots on its body that look like its eggs and has strong muscular legs. It was also observed that insect W feeds on the eggs of animal F.

(a) Explain how the spots on animal F’s body and its muscular legs together increase the number of eggs of animal F that hatch into young.  [2]

It was also observed that another insect Z lays its eggs among animal F’s eggs. The larvae of insect Z feed on the unhatched eggs of animal F. Study graph P and graph Q below.

(b) Which graph, P or Q, correctly shows the duration of the egg stages of animals F and Z such that the larvae of Z have more food? Explain your answer.  [2]

Answer

(a) The spots on male F’s body look like eggs, so when insect W approaches to eat the eggs, it may be confused and try to eat the spots on F’s body instead. Male F then uses its strong muscular legs to kick/drive away insect W. As a result, fewer real eggs are eaten by insect W, so more eggs can hatch successfully.

(b) Graph P is correct. In Graph P, the egg stage of Z is shorter than the egg stage of animal F (Z hatches faster). This means Z’s larvae hatch before F’s eggs hatch. The newly hatched Z larvae can then feed on F’s unhatched eggs, giving Z larvae more food.

Question 32

3 marks

Tom set up an experiment using three similar leaves P, Q and R on the same plant. These leaves have tiny openings on both their upper and lower surfaces. Leaves lose water as water vapour through these tiny openings.

(a) Other than losing water, state one other function of the tiny openings on a leaf.  [1]

He coated some surfaces of the leaves with oil as shown in the table below.

LeafUpper surface coated with oil?Lower surface coated with oil?
PYesNo
QNoYes
RNoNo

The plant was placed under bright sunlight for two hours. Tom measured the volume of water lost at regular intervals. His results are shown in the graph below.

Tom concluded that there are more tiny openings on the upper surface than the lower surface of the leaves.

(b) With reference to the data in the graph and table, explain how Tom arrived at this conclusion.  [2]

Answer

(a) To allow gas exchange: taking in carbon dioxide and releasing oxygen during photosynthesis / taking in oxygen and releasing carbon dioxide during respiration.

(b) From the graph, leaf Q (upper surface not coated, lower surface coated) lost more water than leaf P (upper surface coated, lower surface not coated). Since Q loses water only through its upper surface and P loses water only through its lower surface, the upper surface must have more tiny openings than the lower surface, allowing more water to escape through Q than through P.

Question 33

2 marks

Study the diagram below.

(a) Identify a gas which is at a higher amount in the blood at K than L.  [1]

(b) Other than the parts given in the diagram, state all the other part(s) of the circulatory system.  [1]

Answer

(a) Carbon dioxide (CO&sub2;). Blood travelling along K (from the heart to the lungs) has not yet been oxygenated, so it carries more carbon dioxide than the blood at L (which has already been oxygenated at the lungs and is returning to the heart).

(b) Blood and blood vessels (veins, arteries, and capillaries).

Question 34

3 marks

The diagrams below show the circulatory system and the digestive system in the human body.

(a) Explain how the digestive system and the circulatory system work together to provide nutrients to all parts of the body.  [2]

(b) Explain why a whole piece of meat takes a longer time to digest than minced meat.  [1]

Answer

(a) The digestive system breaks down food into simpler substances (nutrients). These simpler substances are absorbed into the blood at the small intestine. The circulatory system then transports this nutrient-rich blood to all parts of the body, delivering nutrients to all cells.

(b) A whole piece of meat has less surface area in contact with digestive juices than minced meat. With less surface area exposed, the digestive juices act on the meat more slowly, so digestion takes longer.

Question 35

5 marks

The diagram below shows a toy boat with a sail. When Ben turned on the fan at the lowest speed, the wind from the fan caused the toy boat to move forward from point X to point Y.

At point Y, the fan was switched off, but the toy boat continued to move forward before stopping at point Z.

(a) State the source of energy that caused the toy boat to move.  [1]

(b) In terms of energy, give a reason why the toy boat continued to move forward from point Y to point Z.  [1]

(bii) In terms of energy, explain why the toy boat stopped moving at point Z.  [1]

(c) Using the same equipment in the setup, suggest one way to make the toy boat move a longer distance. Give a reason for your answer in terms of energy.  [2]

Suggestion: _______________________________________________

Reason: ___________________________________________________

Answer

(a) Wind energy / kinetic energy of the moving air.

(b) When the fan was switched off at Y, the toy boat still had kinetic energy (it was still moving), so it continued to move forward.

(bii) At point Z, all the kinetic energy of the toy boat has been converted to heat energy and sound energy (due to friction between the boat and the water surface, and air resistance), so the boat has no more kinetic energy to keep moving.

(c) Suggestion (any one): Increase the fan speed / Place the boat nearer to the fan / Make the sail larger / Give the boat a push at the start.
Reason: The toy boat will have more kinetic energy at point X/Y, so it can travel further before all its kinetic energy is used up by friction.

Question 36

4 marks

Trisha conducted an experiment with three similar ramps X, Y and Z as shown in the diagram below. She placed different numbers of strips of the same length and thickness across ramps Y and Z. She released a ball from the top of the ramp and recorded the time taken for the ball to reach the bottom of the ramp.

She repeated the experiment with ramps Y and Z. The readings are recorded in the table below.

RampTime taken for the ball to reach the bottom of the ramp (seconds)
X2
Y5
Z8

(a) What is the relationship between the number of strips and the time taken for the ball to reach the bottom of the ramp?  [1]

(b) Identify the two forces acting on the ball as it rolls down the ramp.  [1]

The diagram below shows a slope at the side of Trisha’s house.

(c) Based on the results above, give a reason in terms of forces, how placing more strips on the slope will prevent Trisha from slipping on a rainy day.  [1]

(d) How can Trisha increase the reliability of the results of her experiment?  [1]

Answer

(a) As the number of strips increases, the time taken for the ball to reach the bottom of the ramp increases.

(b) Friction (frictional force) and gravity (gravitational force).

(c) More strips on the slope create more friction between Trisha’s feet and the slope. The greater friction acts against the tendency to slip downward, preventing Trisha from slipping.

(d) Repeat the experiment at least 3 times for each ramp and calculate the average time / check that results are consistent. This reduces the effect of experimental error.

Question 37

3 marks

Mdm Tan set up a circuit using two identical bulbs, P and Q, and three identical batteries as shown in the diagram below. All the bulbs and batteries are in good working conditions. The metal rod can swing freely about the pivot.

The diagram above shows the metal rod not touching point B.

(a) Put a tick in the box below to indicate the expected observation.  [1]

Did the bulb light up?YesNo
Bulb P✓
Bulb Q✓

When Mdm Tan pressed the button down, the metal spring will be compressed and the metal rod will move up and touch point A.

(b) State and explain the change in her observation for each bulb when she pressed the button.  [2]

(i) Change observed for Bulb P: _______________________________________________

(ii) Change observed for Bulb Q: _______________________________________________

Answer

(a) (as shown in the table above) Bulb P: Yes (lights up); Bulb Q: No (does not light up).

(b)(i) Bulb P becomes brighter. When the button is pressed, more batteries are connected into the circuit, so there is more electric current flowing through Bulb P, making it shine brighter.

(b)(ii) Bulb Q lights up. When the button is pressed and the metal rod touches point A, the circuit path through Bulb Q is now closed / completed, allowing electric current to flow through Bulb Q.

Question 38

4 marks

Evangeline has three puppets, X, Y and Z, of the same height as shown in the diagram below.

(a) Which property of light enables us to see the three puppets?  [1]

She placed the three puppets between a torch and a screen and the shadows of the puppets cast on the screen are as follows.

(b) How are shadows formed?  [1]

(c) Based on the shadows cast on the screen above, which puppet, X, Y or Z, was placed furthest from the torch? Give a reason for your answer.  [1]

(d) Without changing the positions of the three puppets, suggest one change that can be made to increase the size of all the three shadows cast on the screen.  [1]

Answer

(a) Light can be reflected. (Light reflects off the surface of the puppets and enters our eyes, allowing us to see them.)

(b) Shadows are formed when light (travelling in straight lines) is blocked by an opaque object, creating a dark area on the other side where light cannot reach.

(c) Puppet Y. Its shadow is the smallest. A puppet placed furthest from the torch (and closest to the screen) casts the smallest shadow, because the light rays have less divergence before reaching it.

(d) Move the torch nearer to the puppets. OR Move the screen further away from the puppets.

Question 39

4 marks

Jim used a bar magnet to stroke an unmagnetised steel bar XY to magnetise it. The result is shown in the diagram below.

(a) What property of steel bar XY is shown in the diagram?  [1]

Jim also magnetised a steel bar CD using two magnets as shown below.

(b) Using the information from the diagram, state the poles of steel bar CD at C and D.  [1]

Jim wanted to magnetise steel bar EF using the South poles of two magnets as shown below.

(c) Explain, using properties of magnets, why steel bar EF cannot be magnetised using the method above.  [2]

Answer

(a) Steel bar XY is magnetic (it can be magnetised / it is attracted to a magnet / it is a magnetic material).

(b) At C: South pole    At D: North pole.
(The N poles of the two magnets, stroking outward from the center, induce the opposite poles at the ends: South at C and North at D.)

(c) Using the South poles at both ends would induce North poles at both ends E and F of the bar. A magnet must have two different poles — one North and one South. Since both E and F would become North poles (the same pole), the bar cannot form a proper magnet. Accept: Both E and F will become North poles. Magnets should have one North and one South pole (two unlike poles).

Question 40

4 marks

Ethan set up the experiment as shown in the diagram below.

He placed the same amount of ice cubes into each of the three containers and measured the time taken for all the ice to melt completely. He recorded the readings in the table below.

MaterialTime taken for all the ice cubes to melt (min)
F12
G18
H27

(a) What was the aim of his experiment?  [1]

(b) Give a reason why Ethan used the same amount of ice cubes in the three set-ups.  [1]

Ethan wants to bring some hot chicken soup to his mother’s house.

(c) Based on the results above, explain which material — F, G or H — is most suitable for making a container to keep the chicken soup hot for a longer time.  [2]

Answer

(a) To find out how different materials affect the time taken for the ice cubes to melt completely. OR To find out which material is the best (or poorest) conductor of heat.

(b) So that the result (time for ice to melt) is only caused by the different materials used / to make it a fair test, ensuring the amount of ice does not affect the results.

(c) Material H is most suitable. The ice cubes in container H took the longest time to melt (27 min), which shows that material H conducts heat the most slowly. Therefore material H is the poorest conductor of heat. A container made of material H will conduct heat from the hot soup to the surroundings the most slowly, keeping the soup hot for the longest time.
